topic: India denies proposal for long-term tax relief for domestic equity investors
headline: NO TAX RELIEF FOR DOMESTIC EQUITY INVESTORS, GOVT CONFIRMS NO CHANGE IN 12.5% CAPITAL GAINS TAX RATE
summary: India's finance ministry has confirmed that there is no proposal to remove the long-term capital gains tax on equities for domestic investors. Domestic investors will continue to pay a 12.5% tax on qualifying equity gains. The recent tax easing only applies to foreign portfolio investors in government debt. The tax rate remains unchanged for domestic investors.

sections.paragraph: India's economic expansion during the 17th century led to increased patronage of various industries, including painting, textiles, and architecture. The Mughal rule facilitated the rise of new Indian commercial and political elites, particularly along the coasts of southern and eastern India. However, the 19th century saw economic setbacks, including large-scale famines and limited industrial employment for Indians, despite infrastructure development. The introduction of railways provided critical famine relief, reduced the cost of moving goods, and supported nascent Indian-owned industry. By the late 20th century, India's economy had diversified, with the service sector making up 55.6% of GDP, the industrial sector 26.3%, and the agricultural sector 18.1% as of 2017. The country's foreign exchange remittances and external trade have also grown significantly, with India becoming the world's ninth-largest importer and sixteenth-largest exporter by 2021.

sources.title: India - Simple English Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

sections.paragraph: The business impact of recent tax developments in India has been a subject of discussion, with Non-Resident Indians (NRIs) able to park their Indian income in NRO fixed deposits offering attractive interest rates of up to 6.75 percent. However, domestic investors will continue to pay a 12.5% Long-Term Capital Gains (LTCG) tax on qualifying equity gains, while foreign portfolio investors are exempt from this tax on government securities. This move aims to attract stable, long-term foreign capital into India. Despite this, foreign equity portfolio investors have pulled out over Rs 2 lakh crore so far in 2026, and the country is facing a rare discomfort of the third consecutive year of balance-of-payments deficit. To win back foreign investors, some argue that India needs tax reform, including revisiting FDI ownership caps and allowing 100 per cent automatic foreign investment into remaining sectors. Others suggest that the government should focus on curbing imports and reforming subsidies to improve the business environment.

sources.title: To win back foreign investors, India needs tax reform

sections.paragraph: The Indian government has introduced several policy changes, including increasing the tax rate for equity investments held for less than a year to 20 percent and for those held longer than 12 months to 12.5 percent. Additionally, the government has increased the tax on equity derivative transactions, which will be implemented from October 1. The government has also made concessions to its allies, such as hastening loans from multilateral agencies for the eastern state of Bihar and the southern state of Andhra Pradesh. Furthermore, India's tax sovereignty is being tested in the case of the Tiger Global verdict, which involves the taxation of a $16 billion deal and the application of the General Anti-Avoidance Rule (GAAR). The case has implications for India's ability to defend its tax base and has been closely watched by the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD). The Indian government's policies and court rulings on tax matters, such as the Authority for Advance Rulings (AAR) and the Delhi High Court, are shaping the country's tax landscape.

sections.paragraph: India's finance ministry has confirmed that there is no proposal to remove the long-term capital gains tax on equities for domestic investors, which currently stands at 12.5%. This clarification comes after the government recently eased tax rules for foreign portfolio investors in government debt, exempting them from long-term capital gains tax on investments in government securities. The exemption for foreign investors is aimed at attracting stable, long-term foreign capital into India. Domestic investors will continue to pay the 12.5% tax on qualifying equity gains, with some sources noting that the tax rate for domestic and retail investors is the same as for foreign portfolio investors for investments in equity. The government's decision aims to support the rupee and revive overseas flows, which have been impacted by factors such as elevated crude prices and the rupee's slide to record lows. Meanwhile, foreign investors have recently turned net buyers of Indian equities, following a period of large outflows, prompted by favorable domestic macroeconomic trends and improved global risk sentiment.
